agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestrated saga pattern for a courier marketplace order flow spanning 4 services (Order, Inventory, Payment, Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and triggers compensating actions on failure, ensuring all-or-nothing semantics without distributed transactions. Each service owns its local state and exposes both an action and a compensation endpoint. The orchestrator persists saga state to survive crashes, enabling reliable recovery.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga forward through each step, persists saga state (step + status), and triggers compensating actions in reverse order on failure. Single source of truth for saga progress.","boundaries":"Knows the saga definition (step order, action endpoints, compensation endpoints) but contains zero business logic. Does not access any service's database directly."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), confirms orders (CONFIRMED), and cancels orders (CANCELLED) as a compensation. Owns the orders table.","boundaries":"Owns order lifecycle state. Exposes CreateOrder and CancelOrder (compensation) operations."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory items for an order and releases reservations as compensation. Owns inventory and reservation tables.","boundaries":"Owns stock levels and reservation records. Exposes ReserveItems and ReleaseReservation (compensation) operations."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges the customer for an order and issues refunds as compensation. Owns payment transaction records.","boundaries":"Owns payment state. Exposes ChargeCustomer and RefundPayment (compensation) operations."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order and unassigns as compensation. Owns courier availability and assignment records.","boundaries":"Owns courier assignments. Exposes AssignCourier and UnassignCourier (compensation) operations."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instances: current step, status (RUNNING, COMPENSATING, COMPLETED, FAILED), and per-step results (IDs needed for compensation). Enables crash recovery.","boundaries":"Append-only log or table. Orchestrator is the sole writer. Can be a dedicated database table or an event log."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"CreateOrder(orderId, items, customerId) → {orderId, status}; CancelOrder(orderId) → {status}","coupling_type":"temporal (sync request/reply via async messaging preferred)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"ReserveItems(orderId, items[]) → {reservationId}; ReleaseReservation(reservationId) → {status}","coupling_type":"temporal"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"ChargeCustomer(orderId, customerId, amount) → {paymentId}; RefundPayment(paymentId) → {status}","coupling_type":"temporal"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"AssignCourier(orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {assignmentId}; UnassignCourier(assignmentId) → {status}","coupling_type":"temporal"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Saga State Store","contract":"SaveStep(sagaId, step, status, resultData); LoadSaga(sagaId) → SagaState","coupling_type":"data ownership"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order → Orchestrator creates saga (state: STEP_1_ORDER). 2. Orchestrator calls OrderService.CreateOrder → stores orderId, advances to STEP_2_INVENTORY. 3. Orchestrator calls InventoryService.ReserveItems → stores reservationId, advances to STEP_3_PAYMENT. 4. Orchestrator calls PaymentService.ChargeCustomer → stores paymentId, advances to STEP_4_COURIER. 5. Orchestrator calls CourierService.AssignCourier → stores assignmentId, marks saga COMPLETED. On failure at any step N, orchestrator marks saga COMPENSATING and walks backward from step N-1 to step 1, calling each compensation. After all compensations succeed, saga is marked FAILED. Compensation results are also persisted per-step.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestrated Saga","rationale":"Central orchestrator provides clear visibility into saga state, simpler debugging than choreography, and explicit control over compensation ordering. For a linear 4-step flow, orchestration avoids the implicit coupling and tracing difficulty of event choreography."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transaction","rationale":"Each forward action has a paired undo operation. Since distributed ACID transactions are not feasible across 4 services, compensating transactions provide semantic rollback at the application level."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every action and compensation must be idempotent (keyed by sagaId + step). Network retries, orchestrator restarts, and at-least-once delivery all demand safe re-execution without side effects."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Model the saga as an explicit state machine with well-defined transitions. Prevents illegal states, simplifies recovery logic, and makes the saga testable as a pure state machine."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern (recommended)","rationale":"Services should use transactional outbox to atomically persist local state changes and publish events/replies, preventing the dual-write problem between local DB and message broker."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, status: PENDING|CONFIRMED|CANCELLED, items[], customerId)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Reservation (root: reservationId, orderId, items[], status: HELD|RELEASED)","StockItem (root: sku, availableQuantity)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: paymentId, orderId, amount, status: CHARGED|REFUNDED)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: assignmentId, orderId, courierId, status: ASSIGNED|UNASSIGNED)","Courier (root: courierId, availability, location)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"generic","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, orderId, currentStep, status: RUNNING|COMPENSATING|COMPLETED|FAILED, stepResults{})"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Orchestrator tells Order Service what to do; Order Service can reject)"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestrated saga over choreography-based saga","gains":"Centralized state makes debugging, monitoring, and recovery straightforward. Compensation order is explicit. Easy to add/remove/reorder steps. Single place to look for saga status.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by HA deployment). Services are coupled to the orchestrator's protocol. Slightly more infrastructure than pure event-driven choreography.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and listens for others. Avoids central coordinator but creates implicit coupling, harder to trace failures, and compensation ordering becomes distributed and error-prone for a linear flow.","Two-phase commit (2PC): provides true ACID but requires all services to support XA, introduces tight coupling and availability problems. Not viable across independently deployed microservices."]},{"decision":"Synchronous request-reply over async messaging for orchestrator-to-service communication","gains":"Simpler mental model, easier to implement initially, natural request/response pairing.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling — if a service is down, the saga blocks. Reduced resilience compared to async.","alternatives_considered":["Async command/reply via message broker: higher resilience, supports retries and dead-letter queues natively, but adds messaging infrastructure complexity. Recommended for production but sync is acceptable for MVP."]},{"decision":"Persist saga state to a durable store after each step","gains":"Crash recovery — orchestrator can resume or compensate on restart. Audit trail for debugging.","sacrifices":"Adds latency (one write per step). Requires a reliable data store for the orchestrator.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory only saga state: faster but loses all saga progress on crash, leading to orphaned reservations and charges. Unacceptable for a payment-involving flow."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Step ordering and composition","evolution_path":"The saga definition is a declarative list of (action, compensation) pairs. Adding a new step (e.g., Notification Service, Fraud Check) requires only adding an entry — no structural change to the orchestrator."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration","evolution_path":"Orchestrator-to-service communication can migrate from sync HTTP to async messaging (e.g., command/reply queues) without changing the saga state machine logic. The transport is behind an adapter."},{"area":"Compensation policies","evolution_path":"Compensation logic per step can evolve independently (e.g., partial refunds, retry-then-compensate). Each service owns its compensation semantics."},{"area":"Timeout and retry policies","evolution_path":"Per-step timeout and retry configuration can be externalized. Dead-letter handling can be added incrementally."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering must be strictly reverse","reason":"Business invariant: you cannot refund a payment that was never charged, or release a reservation that was never made. The reverse-order compensation is a hard constraint, not a preference."},{"area":"Saga state transitions","reason":"The state machine transitions (RUNNING → COMPENSATING → FAILED, or RUNNING → COMPLETED) are fixed invariants. Allowing arbitrary transitions would corrupt saga state and cause double-charges or orphaned reservations."},{"area":"Idempotency requirement on all operations","reason":"At-least-once delivery is a fundamental property of any reliable distributed system. Removing idempotency would make the system unsafe under retries and crashes. This is non-negotiable."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure — a compensating action itself fails (e.g., refund API is down)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement retry with exponential backoff for compensations. After max retries, persist to a dead-letter/manual-intervention queue. Alert operations team. Compensations must be idempotent so retries are safe."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crash mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Saga state is persisted after each step. On restart, a recovery process loads all RUNNING and COMPENSATING sagas and resumes them. Idempotent operations ensure safe re-execution of the current step."},{"risk":"Semantic inconsistency window — between a failure and completed compensation, the system is temporarily inconsistent","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Accept this as inherent to sagas (eventual consistency). Ensure downstream reads check saga status. Add a COMPENSATING state visible to queries so users see 'cancelling' not 'confirmed'."},{"risk":"Dual-write problem — service updates local DB but fails to send reply to orchestrator","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use the transactional outbox pattern: write local state change and outgoing message/reply in the same local transaction. A relay process publishes from the outbox to the message broker."},{"risk":"Long-running saga holds inventory reservation indefinitely","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Reservations should have a TTL. Inventory service auto-releases reservations past their TTL. Orchestrator must complete or compensate within this window. Monitor saga duration with alerts on breach."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga as an explicit state machine. Define the saga as a list of steps, each with: (1) service name, (2) action command, (3) compensation command, (4) result key to store. The orchestrator loop: load current step → execute action → persist result + advance step → repeat. On failure: set status COMPENSATING → walk steps backward → execute compensation using stored result keys → persist each compensation result. Final state: COMPLETED or FAILED.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/saga/OrderSagaDefinition — declares the 4 steps with action/compensation pairs","src/saga/SagaOrchestrator — generic engine: executes any saga definition, manages state transitions","src/saga/SagaState — entity: sagaId, orderId, currentStep, status, stepResults map","src/saga/SagaStateRepository — persistence for SagaState (one table: saga_instances)","src/saga/SagaRecoveryJob — on startup or periodic schedule, resumes stuck sagas","src/order-service/handlers/CreateOrderHandler, CancelOrderHandler — action + compensation","src/inventory-service/handlers/ReserveItemsHandler, ReleaseReservationHandler","src/payment-service/handlers/ChargeCustomerHandler, RefundPaymentHandler","src/courier-service/handlers/AssignCourierHandler, UnassignCourierHandler"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service handler must be idempotent — use sagaId + stepIndex as an idempotency key. Persist saga state after every step transition. Compensations run in strict reverse order. Use a discriminated union or enum for saga status to prevent illegal transitions. Keep the saga orchestrator generic so it can run any saga definition, not just the order saga.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not mix business logic into the orchestrator — it should only coordinate, never decide business rules","Do not skip persisting saga state between steps — in-memory-only state will cause orphaned charges on crash","Do not assume compensations always succeed — build retry and dead-letter mechanisms from day one","Do not use synchronous distributed transactions (2PC) as a shortcut — it will fail under partial availability","Do not let services call each other directly during the saga — all communication flows through the orchestrator","Do not forget to handle the case where the saga orchestrator itself is restarted mid-compensation — compensation must also be resumable","Do not create a generic 'saga table' that stores untyped JSON blobs — type the step results so compensation has reliable data"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestrated saga is the right pattern for a linear 4-step flow with strict compensation ordering","Compensating transaction design with reverse-order execution","Idempotency and crash recovery requirements","State machine modeling of saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Sync vs async transport choice depends on existing infrastructure and latency requirements not specified","Exact message broker technology and outbox implementation details depend on tech stack"],"assumptions":["Services are independently deployed microservices with their own databases","No existing distributed transaction infrastructure (XA/2PC) is in place","At-least-once delivery semantics are achievable via the chosen transport","Each service can implement idempotent operations keyed by saga context"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Orchestrated saga pattern for a courier marketplace order flow spanning 4 services (Order, Inventory, Payment, Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and triggers compensating actions on failure, ensuring all-or-nothing semantics without distributed transactions. Each service owns its local state and exposes both an action and a compensation endpoint. The orchestrator persists saga state to survive crashes, enabling reliable recovery.","architecture":

Client submits order → Orchestrator creates saga (state: STEP_1_ORDER). 2. Orchestrator calls OrderService.CreateOrder → stores orderId, advances to STEP_2_INVENTORY. 3. Orchestrator calls InventoryService.ReserveItems → stores reservationId, advances to STEP_3_PAYMENT. 4. Orchestrator calls PaymentService.ChargeCustomer → stores paymentId, advances to STEP_4_COURIER. 5. Orchestrator calls CourierService.AssignCourier → stores assignmentId, marks saga COMPLETED. On failure at any step N, orchestrator marks saga COMPENSATING and walks backward from step N-1 to step 1, calling each compensation. After all compensations succeed, saga is marked FAILED. Compensation results are also persisted per-step.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestrated Saga","rationale":"Central orchestrator provides clear visibility into saga state, simpler debugging than choreography, and explicit control over compensation ordering. For a linear 4-step flow, orchestration avoids the implicit coupling and tracing difficulty of event choreography."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transaction","rationale":"Each forward action has a paired undo operation. Since distributed ACID transactions are not feasible across 4 services, compensating transactions provide semantic rollback at the application level."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every action and compensation must be idempotent (keyed by sagaId + step). Network retries, orchestrator restarts, and at-least-once delivery all demand safe re-execution without side effects."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Model the saga as an explicit state machine with well-defined transitions. Prevents illegal states, simplifies recovery logic, and makes the saga testable as a pure state machine."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern (recommended)","rationale":"Services should use transactional outbox to atomically persist local state changes and publish events/replies, preventing the dual-write problem between local DB and message broker."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, status: PENDING|CONFIRMED|CANCELLED, items[], customerId)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Reservation (root: reservationId, orderId, items[], status: HELD|RELEASED)","StockItem (root: sku, availableQuantity)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: paymentId, orderId, amount, status: CHARGED|REFUNDED)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: assignmentId, orderId, courierId, status: ASSIGNED|UNASSIGNED)","Courier (root: courierId, availability, location)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"generic","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, orderId, currentStep, status: RUNNING|COMPENSATING|COMPLETED|FAILED, stepResults{})"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Orchestrator tells Order Service what to do; Order Service can reject)"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestrated saga over choreography-based saga","gains":"Centralized state makes debugging, monitoring, and recovery straightforward. Compensation order is explicit. Easy to add/remove/reorder steps. Single place to look for saga status.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by HA deployment). Services are coupled to the orchestrator's protocol. Slightly more infrastructure than pure event-driven choreography.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and listens for others. Avoids central coordinator but creates implicit coupling, harder to trace failures, and compensation ordering becomes distributed and error-prone for a linear flow.","Two-phase commit (2PC): provides true ACID but requires all services to support XA, introduces tight coupling and availability problems. Not viable across independently deployed microservices."]},{"decision":"Synchronous request-reply over async messaging for orchestrator-to-service communication","gains":"Simpler mental model, easier to implement initially, natural request/response pairing.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling — if a service is down, the saga blocks. Reduced resilience compared to async.","alternatives_considered":["Async command/reply via message broker: higher resilience, supports retries and dead-letter queues natively, but adds messaging infrastructure complexity. Recommended for production but sync is acceptable for MVP."]},{"decision":"Persist saga state to a durable store after each step","gains":"Crash recovery — orchestrator can resume or compensate on restart. Audit trail for debugging.","sacrifices":"Adds latency (one write per step). Requires a reliable data store for the orchestrator.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory only saga state: faster but loses all saga progress on crash, leading to orphaned reservations and charges. Adding a new step (e.g., Notification Service, Fraud Check) requires only adding an entry — no structural change to the orchestrator."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration","evolution_path":"Orchestrator-to-service communication can migrate from sync HTTP to async messaging (e.g., command/reply queues) without changing the saga state machine logic. Each service owns its compensation semantics."},{"area":"Timeout and retry policies","evolution_path":"Per-step timeout and retry configuration can be externalized. Dead-letter handling can be added incrementally."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering must be strictly reverse","reason":"Business invariant: you cannot refund a payment that was never charged, or release a reservation that was never made. The reverse-order compensation is a hard constraint, not a preference."},{"area":"Saga state transitions","reason":"The state machine transitions (RUNNING → COMPENSATING → FAILED, or RUNNING → COMPLETED) are fixed invariants. Allowing arbitrary transitions would corrupt saga state and cause double-charges or orphaned reservations."},{"area":"Idempotency requirement on all operations","reason":"At-least-once delivery is a fundamental property of any reliable distributed system. Removing idempotency would make the system unsafe under retries and crashes. This is non-negotiable."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure — a compensating action itself fails (e.g., refund API is down)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement retry with exponential backoff for compensations. Compensations must be idempotent so retries are safe."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crash mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Saga state is persisted after each step. On restart, a recovery process loads all RUNNING and COMPENSATING sagas and resumes them. Idempotent operations ensure safe re-execution of the current step."},{"risk":"Semantic inconsistency window — between a failure and completed compensation, the system is temporarily inconsistent","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Accept this as inherent to sagas (eventual consistency). Ensure downstream reads check saga status. Add a COMPENSATING state visible to queries so users see 'cancelling' not 'confirmed'."},{"risk":"Dual-write problem — service updates local DB but fails to send reply to orchestrator","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use the transactional outbox pattern: write local state change and outgoing message/reply in the same local transaction. Inventory service auto-releases reservations past their TTL. Orchestrator must complete or compensate within this window. Monitor saga duration with alerts on breach."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga as an explicit state machine. Define the saga as a list of steps, each with: (1) service name, (2) action command, (3) compensation command, (4) result key to store. The orchestrator loop: load current step → execute action → persist result + advance step → repeat. On failure: set status COMPENSATING → walk steps backward → execute compensation using stored result keys → persist each compensation result. Keep the saga orchestrator generic so it can run any saga definition, not just the order saga.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not mix business logic into the orchestrator — it should only coordinate, never decide business rules","Do not skip persisting saga state between steps — in-memory-only state will cause orphaned charges on crash","Do not assume compensations always succeed — build retry and dead-letter mechanisms from day one","Do not use synchronous distributed transactions (2PC) as a shortcut — it will fail under partial availability","Do not let services call each other directly during the saga — all communication flows through the orchestrator","Do not forget to handle the case where the saga orchestrator itself is restarted mid-compensation — compensation must also be resumable","Do not create a generic 'saga table' that stores untyped JSON blobs — type the step results so compensation has reliable data"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestrated saga is the right pattern for a linear 4-step flow with strict compensation ordering","Compensating transaction design with reverse-order execution","Idempotency and crash recovery requirements","State machine modeling of saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Sync vs async transport choice depends on existing infrastructure and latency requirements not specified","Exact message broker technology and outbox implementation details depend on tech stack"],"assumptions":["Services are independently deployed microservices with their own databases","No existing distributed transaction infrastructure (XA/2PC) is in place","At-least-once delivery semantics are achievable via the chosen transport","Each service can implement idempotent operations keyed by saga context"]}}
